passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.heading: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
Listen:

I have no answer to your question

I am not kidding when I tell you:

I earned my own voice

The shape it makes in the world holds meKazim Ali, Origin Story

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Box: Learning objectives
passages.heading: Learning objectives
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
- Understand why necessary and sufficient conditions fail for linguistic definitions

- Distinguish everyday from technical meanings of grammatical terms

- Apply critical thinking strategies including looking for counter-examples

- Begin to identify the basic lexical categories of English: nouns, verbs, adjectives, and determinatives

- Recognize how words can belong to different categories in different contexts

- Understand the concept of lexical categories as prototype-based rather than rule-based

- Apply systematic criteria to identify lexical categories despite boundary cases

- Appreciate the complexity underlying seemingly simple grammatical concepts

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction
passages.heading: Introduction
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
If the idea of teaching English grammar makes you nervous, you're not alone. Many aspiring English-language teachers feel intimidated by grammar, unsure about the difference between an adverb and an adjective, or worried they don't know enough about the intricacies of the language they hope to teach. But here's a secret: understanding grammar isn't about memorizing a set of rigid rules. There will, of course, be memorization, but that won't be of much use unless we develop new ways of thinking about language, ways that will help us make sense of English and, eventually, help your future students do the same.

This chapter is designed to ease you into this new way of thinking. I'm not going to start by bombarding you with entirely unfamiliar terms like clitic, deixis, or ergative, and I won't be setting out any complex rules. Instead, we'll begin by questioning some basic assumptions about language and exploring how we define the things we think we understand.

We'll start with some fundamental concepts and gradually build up to more specific grammatical ideas. Along the way, we'll develop critical thinking strategies that are crucial for anyone planning to teach language. You'll learn to look for counter-examples, to consider different perspectives, and to recognize the difference between everyday and technical uses of terms.

If you're skeptical that there's any value at all in knowing a lot about English, again, you're in good company. But research on Teacher Language Awareness (TLA) suggests that teachers who understand how language works as a system are better equipped to diagnose learner errors, anticipate difficulty, and adapt their explanations on the fly (Andrews, 2007). As Stevick puts it, the teacher needs to know the pathways and obstacles of the language not only to run back and forth in them for herself, but also to see them as they look to a newcomer (1980: 251, cited in (Andrews, 2007): 7). Don't get me wrong: this book is not about becoming able to teach linguistics to your students. It's about what you, as a teacher, need to know in order to anticipate their problems, respond to them, and put into words what fluent speakers do without thinking.

But for something to count as knowledge, it has to be true, and much of what passes for knowledge about language fails this test. Nouns are people, places, and things; verbs are action words - these are beliefs about language, not knowledge. Bartels (2009) cautions that academic knowledge about language doesn't automatically transfer to classroom practice. I'd say that's partly because it doesn't qualify as knowledge. True knowledge about language does become useful when teachers connect it to the demands of their classrooms, and this book includes many exercises that help you make those connections.

Don't worry if you feel unsure about your grammar knowledge right now. By the end of this chapter, you'll have a basic toolkit for thinking about language that will help you approach grammar with more confidence. And we'll start with a question that might seem simple at first, but will challenge us to think more deeply: What makes soup soup?

It seems like a silly, irrelevant, and trivially easy question, at least until you try to answer it. What's the difference between tea and soup, sauce and soup, porridge and soup, stew and soup, juice and soup? Don't forget that there's gazpacho, salmorejo, cucumber soup, and other cold soups. (Source: BBC Scotland (https://youtu.be/Y1HVTNxwt7w).)

This isn't special to soup. Most things, when you start looking at examples and counter-examples, turn out to be really hard to pin down. The problem is that we try to define things by picking out necessary and sufficient conditions.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > Necessary and sufficient conditions
passages.heading: Necessary and sufficient conditions
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
In both philosophical and scientific circles we find the concept of necessary and sufficient conditions. A necessary condition must be present for acceptance; a sufficient condition, if present, guarantees acceptance. In other words, when we say a condition is necessary and sufficient for a word to be categorized as a noun, we mean that the word can't be a noun unless it possesses characteristic A, and that if characteristic A is present, the word is definitely a noun.

A clear and straightforward example of this is the concept of even numbers in mathematics. An integer is an even number if dividing it by two results in another integer. This is both a necessary and sufficient condition for an integer to be even. No even number exists that is not divisible by two in this way (making it a necessary condition), and any such number is always even (making it a sufficient condition). (Even something as seemingly definitive as the difference between even and odd numbers may not be as clear-cut as we think (Heubner et al., 2018).)

So, from philosophical abstractions to mathematical certainties, necessary and sufficient conditions often frame our understanding of concepts.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
passages.heading: The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
But when we come to language and its components, definitions that use necessary and sufficient conditions tend to fail. Let's say you defined a noun as a word that denotes a person, place, or thing and you defined a verb as an action word. Take a moment to think about what kind of word could be more of an action word than action. And then think about whether action is a verb or a noun (hint: check a dictionary (https://www.ldoceonline.com/)).

To be clear, action is usually a noun, but it's also a verb (e.g., We need to action these tasks before the end of the day.). Or perhaps it would be better to say that there are two actions, the more common one being a noun and the relatively rare one being a verb.

But it's not just action. Consider the definition of violation. (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/violation) It's a noun, and it's an action. The same is true of many nouns, from blow to cue and signal to sin. They are defined using the phrase the action of, so this pretty much destroys the very common idea that a noun is a person, place, or thing and a verb is an action word. Nouns can be action words too.

We define words to understand language, but we need to understand language to define words.

A thinking trap
You see four cards on a table. Each card has a number on one side and a color on the other side. The sides you can see have a 3, an 8, and the colors blue and red. To check the rule if a card has an even number on one side, then the other side is blue, which card(s) should you flip over?

figures/wason.png
figureImage by User:Life of Riley and User:Domdomegg, CC BY-SA 4.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

License: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0

Many people would say the 8 and the blue. And indeed, you need to check the 8. If the other side is not blue, the rule is violated. On the other hand, flipping the blue card doesn't test the rule. The rule says nothing about odd numbers at all, only about even numbers, which is presumably why almost nobody checks the 3 card. So, whether the other side of the blue card is an even or odd number, it doesn't affect the rule. The red card holds the key. Turn it over, and if you find an even number, the rule would be violated.

Our goal is not only to affirm the rule but also to look for counter-examples, situations where the rule fails. This is just as important with the definitions of linguistic concepts. A definition that either includes non-members or excludes members is not a good definition. If we're assessing an action word as a definition for a verb, we need to consider both affirming and contradicting evidence. For example, we can check whether the word run is an action word (it is) and whether it is a verb (it is). On the other hand, we also need to look for contradicting evidence such as words that are considered action words but are not verbs, like action or run in the sentence I went for a run. And words like exist and sense, which are not necessarily perceived as action words, can be verbs.

But maybe we can save something here. Can you think of any words denoting physical objects that are verbs? Spend a few moments.

It's safe to say that nouns and verbs can both denote actions, but verbs never denote physical objects, or people or places, for that matter. We need a noun for that. This leads us to the following conclusion:

It's true that persons, places, and things are only denoted by nouns, but

it's false that nouns only denote persons, places, and things.

That's a subtle difference, but an important one. Nouns can denote a wider variety of things, while verbs are more limited, and the sets of things that nouns and verbs can denote overlap.

So far, we've identified three issues when it comes to defining nouns and verbs. When it comes to adjectives and other lexical categories, similar points apply, but let's take stock right now.

- A simple definition with necessary and sufficient conditions will include things that don't belong (gravy is made from stock but it's not soup) and exclude things that do belong (gazpacho is served cold but it's still soup).

- We can't just make our definition fit the examples we have in mind. We need to consciously think of counter-examples.

- Sometimes we need to restate our ideas to accurately express them.

As language teachers, we use categories like noun and verb a lot. But categorizing words is a tool, not the end goal. The purpose of categorizing is to be able to make general statements about the world or, in our case, about language.

Here's why this matters: the wrong categories lead to more rules and more exceptions than you need. Having the right categories means you can say more with less, and this makes life easier for you and for your students.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions > Box: Questions
passages.heading: Questions
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
- What's the main goal of this course?

- What's wrong with the idea that an English noun is the name of a person, place, or thing?

- Which statement is a necessary and sufficient definition of English verbs: A) verbs denote actions. B) actions are denoted by verbs. C) neither of the above.

- How is the soup video relevant to defining word categories?

- What's the thinking trap we should try to avoid?

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
passages.heading: The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
Answers

- We are gaining facility with the analysis of language so that we can provide our students with the tools that they can use to decide how to speak English.

- In English, nouns denote a far wider range of concepts than this, including actions and characteristics.

- C) is correct. The category of verbs can't be reliably defined based on denotations.

- It illustrates that categories in general don't have necessary and sufficient properties.

- We look only for confirmatory evidence to support our hypotheses, and we fail to look for evidence that might disconfirm them.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > What do we mean by phrase?
passages.heading: What do we mean by phrase?
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
What do we mean by phrase?
passages.text:
Just as we found with nouns and verbs, some of our most basic assumptions about language can trip us up. Take the word phrase, for instance. You probably think you know what it means (after all, we use phrases every day). In everyday language, a phrase is just a group of words that go together to express an idea, like on the table or be going to. And that's a perfectly good sense of phrase. It's the most common one, and the one most people associate with the word.

But there is also a technical sense, and that's the one I'll be using throughout. A phrase in English grammar is a specific type of structure with two key properties:

- It has a head, a core word that determines the basic properties of the whole phrase

- It may have dependents, optional words or other phrases that modify or complete the meaning of the head

This is quite different from the everyday notion of words that go together. For example, coffee and tea might seem like a perfectly good phrase in everyday speech, but technically it's not a single phrase: it's a coordination of two noun phrases (coffee and tea) joined by and (see Section for more about coordinations). None of these words is the head of the whole expression.

Examples of phrases, in the everyday sense, that aren't phrases in the technical sense include:

2

can't help but

let alone

want to try again

pick up the pencil

good or bad

salt and pepper

Examples in ( and b) are missing their required dependents: you can't say this isn't working, let alone. Examples ( and d) show bracketed portions that could form complete phrases (want to try again, pick up the pencil), but we've artificially extracted them from their full contexts.

The coordination examples in ( and f) present a different issue entirely. Unlike the headed structures we've been discussing, coordinations join elements of equal status with no single head. When we say salt and pepper, neither noun licenses the other; they're simply equals. This makes coordinations fundamentally different from phrases in our technical sense, which require a head.

For now, there's no need to think too hard about what a phrase is. I just want to alert you to the fact that I'm going to be using phrase in a way that might seem familiar but is actually subtly different.

This distinction between the technical meaning of phrase and the everyday meaning is not so unusual. Academics often adopt common words but give them a technical meaning. This doesn't mean that the everyday sense is wrong or that the technical one is; it's common for words to have various meanings. But it's useful for English teachers to be aware that many linguistic terms, like phrase, have a technical sense which is similar to but slightly different from the everyday meaning.

This technical definition of phrases will be important throughout the following chapters. When we talk about noun phrases, verb phrases, and other types, we'll always be referring to these headed structures, whether or not they have dependents, not just any group of connected words. To be explicit, this allows for one-word phrases such as those italicized in ().

Stop!

Would you like pepper with that?

With this clearer understanding of what we mean by phrases, we can now turn to the specific types of phrases we find in English, starting with noun phrases and the nouns that head them.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases
passages.heading: Nouns and noun phrases
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
There's no perfect definition of noun, (This book is about English, and so, here and throughout, I mean English nouns, verbs, tenses, etc. When I want to talk about nouns in languages in general, I'll make that clear.) just as there's no perfect definition of soup, but we can get very close, as long as we're willing to hedge with words like can, almost, and typically.

I will use words here that have not yet been explained. This may be frustrating, but it is inevitable. Syntax is a system, and its elements can only be defined in relation to each other. The meaning of the other words will become clear soon, and the Glossary collects all the technical terms in one place.

- Nouns can denote almost anything.

- A word that denotes a person, place, or thing is a noun
(but not a noun is a word that denotes person, place, or thing).

- A word that is countable is a noun
(but not all nouns are countable).

- Almost any word that is plural is a noun (but not all nouns can be plural).

- Nouns often follow the or a (but not words following the or a are nouns).

- Nouns head noun phrases (NPs).

- Noun phrases can typically include adjective phrases as modifiers
(and other types of phrases typically can't).

- Noun phrases can't typically include adverb phrases as modifiers
(but other types of phrases typically can).

- Noun phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object (but not subjects and objects are noun phrases). (Some examples follow, and I'll say more about what subjects and objects are later.)

- Noun phrases can typically be possessive. (The Cambridge grammar of the English language uses the term genitive instead of possessive because the relationship is often not one of possession. The term possessive, though, is much more common, so I will use it here, as long as you promise to remember that your pet's vet is not a vet possessed by your pet.)

Importantly, each of these characteristics can be odd or unfamiliar to English-language learners. In the other languages they speak, there may be no plural nouns, for example. So the elements of the definition point to facts about English nouns that may be useful for English-language learners to learn. It's also important to note that many of the statements are not commutative (multiplication is commutative but division isn't; but ). In other words, the way you say something can be really important.

Unfortunately, people often won't notice (or will later disregard) the difference between nouns can typically follow the or a and a word that follows the or a is typically a noun. In fact, the is followed immediately by a noun about 34,500 times per million words, while it's followed by a non-noun about 48,400 times per million words. (Author's calculations from the Corpus of Contemporary American English (Davies, 2024).) So, while the next word after the is often a noun, anyone relying on this to be true will be wrong more often than they're right.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples
passages.heading: Some examples
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: The following is meant to make the characteristics listed above more concrete by providing examples. After considering each set of examples, try to think of nouns that do NOT have one or more of the properties listed. In other words, look for counter-examples.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > What nouns denote
passages.heading: What nouns denote
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: We've already seen how nouns can denote actions. We also have characteristics (e.g., beauty, happiness, size, aptitude), states (e.g., absence), relations (e.g., togetherness, superiority), and on and on.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Countability
passages.heading: Countability
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: Here are some examples of count nouns: apple, book, chair, pen, tree, giraffe, telescope, flute, cathedral, quasar, enzyme. And here are some non-count nouns: water, information, rice, music, furniture, advice, syrup, flour, machinery, courage, syntax, kinship.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Plural nouns
passages.heading: Plural nouns
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: Consider the following examples of plural nouns: we, women, children, students, things, eyes, police, fish, words, thanks, feet, and problems.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Nouns following the or a
passages.heading: Nouns following the or a
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: These examples are very simple noun phrases with the and a plus a head noun: the world, a lot, the way, the end, the people, a man, the day, a couple, and a year.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Noun phrases with adjective-phrase modifiers and head nouns
passages.heading: Noun phrases with adjective-phrase modifiers and head nouns
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
Noun phrases with adjective-phrase modifiers and head nouns
passages.text: Next, examine these noun phrases with adjective-phrase modifiers and head nouns. The adjective phrase is italicized, and the head noun is in small caps: a long time, a little bit, other people, the twelfth one, the supreme court, a very good morning, the only way I know, an old man, and social media.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Noun phrases functioning as subjects
passages.heading: Noun phrases functioning as subjects
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: In each of the following sentences, the italicized noun phrase is the subject: you win; time is on my side; a little bit goes a long way; other people can help; the supreme court released its decision; often, the twelfth one is perfect; a very good morning is a rare pleasure; on Monday, the shop will be closed; and what do human rights mean.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Possessive noun phrases
passages.heading: Possessive noun phrases
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: The following noun phrases are possessive: my, mine, its, whose, Brett's, time's, other people's, the supreme court's, and the lady who lives here's.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Good examples of nouns
passages.heading: Good examples of nouns
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: A word like school, for instance, has all the properties listed above. It's a prototypical noun, a good example. Here are some other generally good examples of nouns, though they might not each exhibit every property given above: person, way, day, life, man, school, student, thing, house, family, work, part, night, country, government, place, money, water, and room. Which nouns lack which properties?

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns
passages.heading: Less good examples of nouns
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
A word like umbrage, for instance, is missing a number of the properties listed above. For instance, it has no plural, nor do you find possessive NPs with umbrage as the head. It doesn't denote a physical object, a person, or a place. It is not countable and therefore it doesn't follow an. It has other oddities too; can you think of any?

Other not-so-good examples include: contemplation, crockery, furniture, sake (as in for heaven's sake), shrift, what, and behalf. What properties does each of them lack? Practice thinking of nouns that are missing one or more of the properties. Use a good dictionary to check if you're not sure the word is a noun. (I would suggest the Simple English Wiktionary. (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Main_Page) I'm an editor there, and I'll stand behind the category system there. If you don't find the word you're looking for there, then try the Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English. (https://www.ldoceonline.com/))

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns > Box: Questions
passages.heading: Questions
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
From each group of statements, choose the one that is true and relevant. (180[t]0.91. a; 2. a; 3. c; 4. b; 5. a; 6. c
)

-

- Nouns denote almost anything: objects, actions, characteristics.

- A noun is a word that denotes only people, places, and things.

-

- Any word that is countable is a noun.

- You can count nouns.

- All nouns are countable.

-

- A plural noun always ends in an -s.

- Any noun can be plural.

- Almost any word that is plural is a noun.

-

- A noun is any word that follows the or a.

- Nouns can typically follow the or a.

- Any word that typically follows the or a is a noun.

-

- A noun phrase can typically include an adjective phrase as a modifier.

- A noun phrase can typically include an adverb phrase as its modifier.

- Any phrase with an adjective phrase as its modifier is a noun phrase.

-

- Nouns themselves can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object.

- Noun phrases function only as subjects or objects.

- Noun phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns > Box: Find the nouns
passages.heading: Find the nouns
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
In reality, from the top of the ladder, standing erect on the last rung, you could just touch the Moon if you held your arms up. We had taken the measurements carefully (we didn't yet suspect that she was moving away from us); the only thing you had to be very careful about was where you put your hands. I always chose a scale that seemed fast (we climbed up in groups of five or six at a time), then I would cling first with one hand, then with both, and immediately I would feel ladder and boat drifting away from below me, and the motion of the Moon would tear me from the Earth's attraction. Yes, the Moon was so strong that she pulled you up; you realized this the moment you passed from one to the other: you had to swing up abruptly, with a kind of somersault, grabbing the scales, throwing your legs over your head, until your feet were on the Moon's surface. (180[t]0.9
reality, top, ladder, rung, you, Moon, you, your, arms. We, measurements, we, she, us; thing, you, you, your, hands. I, scale, we, groups, time, I, ladder, boat, me, motion, Moon, me, Earth's, attraction. Moon, she, you; you, moment, you, other; you, kind, somersault, scales, your, legs, your, head, your, feet, Moon's, surface.
)
- Italoauthorcalvino2018, The distance of the moon

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Countability
passages.heading: Countability
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
Countability is a remarkably easy concept. If you can say two, where is the noun, then is a count noun. For example, two pencils is fine so pencil is a count noun. But you can't say *There are two furnitures (* means it's ungrammatical), so furniture is a non-count noun. Sometimes, words like jeans seem like they might be count nouns, but it turns out that we count pairs of jeans, not jeans. Another perhaps-surprising example of a non-count noun is police: we don't say *two police were standing on the corner. Instead, we say two (police) officers, where officer is a count noun. Meat is an example of a noun that is not usually countable but can be if we mean 'kinds of meat'. This way of creating count nouns from non-count nouns in a 'kinds-of' meaning is quite common.

The concept of countability may be easy to understand, but the trick is in knowing for which nouns one can or can't say two. It may seem quite obvious to you what is countable and what is not, and you might think that because it's so obvious, it will be very easy to explain to students. You might even have some deep down feeling that somebody who failed to get the concept must be a little slow. But if you have that feeling, it's only because you already know. In fact, even experts can have a very muddled concept of countability. Take, for example, the Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English.

I check this excellent dictionary regularly because it has very informative entries, but if you look up uncountable, (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/uncountable/) you'll find the following definition:

An uncountable noun has no plural form and refers to something which can't be counted or regarded as either singular or plural, for example 'money' or 'happiness'.

Sadly, Longman has fallen into the very thinking trap we considered earlier: They thought up a few matching examples and failed to consider counterexamples. Let's look, on their behalf, for the counterexamples. Can you think of a noun that does have a plural form but which can't be counted, in other words, a plural noun for which you can't say two? We've already considered at least one example in Section. See the footnote after you've given it some thought. (180[t]0.9
We've already seen police. Pants is another example. We say two pairs of pants, not *two pants. Still others include groceries, remains, clothes, scissors, genitals.
) The funny thing is that the dictionary applies these labels correctly to individual words. The entry for glasses, (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/glasses/) for instance, is correctly labeled as uncountable. The editors know that glasses is plural and that it's a non-count noun, but they haven't noticed that their definition is in conflict with their practice.

Why does this matter?
Consider the following:

[]I had (a) tea.

[]I had a cookie.

[*]I had cookie.

I hope you'll agree with me that () is perfectly fine with or without the a, but that in () the a is required and that () is ungrammatical. What could explain this difference? It turns out that singular count nouns require a determiner (a term I'll explain in a later chapter). The words a and the can function as determiners. So can words like one, his, Brett's, every, etc. So, if you have a concept of count vs non-count, and you've got a sense of singular vs plural, you can use that to explain why () is fine either way, but () isn't. This is what I mean when I say that we're working with a system: as you learn about some parts of it, you can use those parts to solve mysteries in other areas that are not obviously connected.

So, we see that the definition of uncountable in the Longman dictionary is wrong, even though it's generally a very good dictionary and even though its own entry for glasses (among many others) contradicts its definition of uncountable. Maybe this determination isn't so easy. But even if creating a good definition turns out to be a little harder than we thought, it may still feel like it should be quite obvious what is countable and uncountable based on certain properties. If that were the case, though, then we would find that singular nouns always translated as singulars and plural nouns always as plurals (at least, when a language has plurals). But in the realm of countability, what counts in one language may not count in another.

If you're multilingual, perhaps you've already run into this. Consider the French:

[]J' ai coupé mes cheveux.

I have cut my-pl hairs-pl.

'I cut my hair.'

In, the French word cheveux is a plural count noun (though it would be rare to count hairs, even in French), while in English it's usually a singular non-count noun. As the French shows, there's no need to enumerate hairs; you could just say I cut my hairs and leave it at that. English treats hair as a mass; French treats it as discrete items. Neither is more logical.

It's easy to think that ideas that we take for granted, such as whether something is countable or not, are objective facts. In many cases like this, they are actually inter-subjective judgments, many entirely unpredictable to those outside the community of speakers of a given language or dialect. Part of the trouble is that countable pulls double duty: it describes a grammatical property of the noun (takes numerals, pluralizes) and a conceptual property of what the noun refers to (individual items vs. undifferentiated stuff). These mostly line up, but when they don't, as with furniture or news, confusion follows. We'll see this kind of double duty again.

And yet, many language-teaching materials treat countability as if it were obvious from mere introspection. Far from it! Consider the following explanation from Allen (2008), a textbook for children. It's perfectly simple if you speak English and almost entirely unhelpful if you don't.

You can see from the list that some nouns can be counted and others can not. For example, if you want to tell your friend about the fruit on the tree, you could tell them your tree has plenty of plums on it. The noun 'plum' is countable so you have to make it plural by adding an '-s-' (sic). Or you could tell them your tree has plenty of fruit on it. In this case, the noun 'fruit' is uncountable, so you can not count an '-s'. Allen (2008)

The book assumes that plums are countable because you can count them, and you can count them because they're countable. What could be easier? What about rice and seed? There is no way for a learner of English to guess that you can say two seeds but not *two rices if you mean 'two grains of rice'. Two rices can only mean 'two kinds of rice', not 'two grains'.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Countability > Box: Practice
passages.heading: Practice
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
For each item, decide whether the noun is singular or plural and whether it is a countable sense of the noun or a non-countable sense.

E.g., The two fish escaped. countable, plural

- The data were accurate.

- The data was accurate.

- The Canadian media are generally reliable.

- The Canadian media is generally reliable.

- The sheep are grazing.

- Little difference could be seen.

- A run will be calming.

- a little sugar

- made of rock

- The remains are buried here.

- More suction, please.

- Pizza is tasty.

- Eating lamb is less common here.

- two or three times

- The children are studying.

- A little knowledge can be dangerous.

- the police

- some new jeans

- two coffees

- new employment statistics

- a little time

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases
passages.heading: Verbs and verb phrases
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
So, what can we say about English verbs? Here's a list of verbs, nice verby verbs, really typical ones:

jump, walk, ask, turn, work, move, play, pull, reach, laugh

Are they action words? Of course they are. Did you notice that this could be a list of nouns though?

She missed the jump.

I went for a walk.

That's a pretty big ask.

Give it a turn and see.

I went to work.

Each of the italicized words above is a noun. (If you doubt me, consider the characteristics we studied earlier.) But in the following cases, they're verbs.

She jumped the gap.

I walk the dog each morning.

He's asking for a 10,000 raise.

It's hard to turn the knob.

I work in Toronto.

So, how can we tell the difference between nouns and verbs? Sometimes we can't until they're in context. In form, they may be the same. In function, they are worlds apart. The list in is perfectly ambiguous. Still, verbs generally have the following properties (examples below):

- Verbs typically denote actions and states, and they don't denote physical objects.

- A word that has a past-tense form is a verb (but not all verbs have past-tense forms).

- Almost every verb has an -s form (but not all).

- Almost every verb has an -ing form (but not all).

- Almost every verb can follow the infinitive marker to.

- A verb can't be modified by an adjective phrase.

- A verb can typically be modified by an adverb phrase.

- A verb phrase must be headed by a verb (always).

- A clause must be headed by a verb phrase (always).

- A verb is often in an agreement relationship with a phrase functioning as the subject of the clause.

Let's look at some examples.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > What verbs denote
passages.heading: What verbs denote
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
These are just everyday classifications, not technical ones, and other types could be added to the list.

Verbs:

actions: come, go, wave, run, swim, play, hit

mental activities: consider, wonder, decide, recall

states: be, seem, own, need, taste, matter, appear, exist

mental states: believe, know, agree, mind, like

accomplishments: win, sleep, become, remain, relax, stop, fail

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Past-tense forms
passages.heading: Past-tense forms
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
Verbs almost always have a past-tense form, while nouns never do. (Can you think of any verbs that lack a past-tense form? (180[t]0.9
Must, beware, and ought are the only verbs that don't have a past-tense form, as far as I know. Verbs that lack one or more forms are said to be defective.
))

Verbs:

Nouns:

There are a lot of nouns and verbs that share a history, spelling, and pronunciation, such as those in. They belong to the same [sec:family-lemma-shape-token]word family, but they are distinct words, in the same way that my brothers and I belong to the same Reynolds family but are, nevertheless, distinct people.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Other verb forms
passages.heading: Other verb forms
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
Almost all verbs also have an -s form and an -ing form, along with their plain form.

run runs running

go goes going

try tries trying

make makes making

do does doing

have has having

contemplate contemplates contemplating

recondition reconditions reconditioning

Can you think of any verbs that lack these forms? (180[t]0.9
Can, may, will, shall, must, and ought are the most common verbs that lack these forms.
)

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Modifiers in verb phrases
passages.heading: Modifiers in verb phrases
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: A verb phrase typically allows an adverb phrase as a modifier but not an adjective phrase. Here are some verb phrases modified by adverb phrases: run quickly, think deeply, try consistently, always be good, easily do it, and happily help. And for comparison, here are some matching noun phrases modified by adjective phrases: a quick run, deep thoughts, consistent effort, an easy goal, and the happy helper.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Verbs head verb phrases
passages.heading: Verbs head verb phrases
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text: Each example is a verb phrase. Parentheses enclose smaller verb phrases inside the larger ones: try, never go to school, had a new idea, usually playing tennis with a friend, have (finished dinner), may (be (leaving soon))

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Verb phrases as heads of clauses
passages.heading: Verb phrases as heads of clauses
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
Verb phrases as heads of clauses
passages.text: Each example is a clause. The verb phrase is italicized. Dogs never go to school. She is usually playing tennis with a friend. Who had a new idea? The folks at table 5 have finished dinner. I may be leaving soon. Try.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Agreement with subject
passages.heading: Agreement with subject
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
In the case of present-tense verbs, if the subject is third-person singular, then the verb typically agrees with the subject by appearing in the -s form. A third-person noun phrase is any one that is not first-person (I, me, myself, we, ours, etc.) or second person (you, yours, yourself).

She studies English.
(She is 3rd person singular, and the -s at the end of the present-tense verb studies marks the agreement.)

She studied English.
(She is 3rd person singular, but the verb is in the past tense.)

They study English.
(They is not singular.)

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Auxiliary verbs
passages.heading: Auxiliary verbs
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
There is a small sub-category of verbs called the auxiliary verbs, shown in Table. They have the NICER properties (Sag et al., 2020).

Table: The NICER properties

These include the modal auxiliary verbs:

will/won't; would/wouldn't

shall/shan't; should/shouldn't

may; might/mightn't

can/can't; could/couldn't

must/mustn't

And a few others:

- be, do (for [ch:questions]interrogatives), and have (for the perfect)

- need, dare, and ought (as modal auxiliaries, though not typically in North America)

Again, these explanations use terms that I haven't yet defined. I understand that it may be frustrating, but it is inevitable. Syntax is a system, and its elements can only be defined in relation to each other. The meanings of the other words will become clear soon. When you meet them, you should cycle back and have another look at this material.

Language learners face this kind of frustration all the time. It's not helpful to say it builds character, but it is true that those who can accept this kind of temporary ambiguity tend to be more successful in the end.

passages.chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Summary
passages.heading: Summary
path: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
passages.text:
In this chapter, along with introducing some key grammatical terms and concepts, I've tried to suggest some mental habits and dispositional attitudes that, I believe, are extremely useful for English-language teachers. These approaches to thinking about English include:

- Seeking counter-examples: This habit pushes us beyond simplistic rules and explanations and helps us to avoid telling our students things that are common knowledge and also wrong.

- Scrutinizing definitions: By examining how we define grammatical concepts, we can better explain them to learners and anticipate their questions.

- Recognizing non-commutativity in language descriptions: Understanding that A implies B doesn't mean B implies A can help us avoid common pitfalls in explanation.

- Challenging our linguistic intuitions: What seems obvious to us as expert English speakers may be opaque to learners. This awareness can help us empathize with our students' struggles.

- Distinguishing between technical and everyday meanings of terms: Such distinctions can help prevent the familiar meanings from confusing our understanding.

An important overarching concept is that language is a system. Each part relies on others, and none can be fully understood in isolation. The more we weave ideas together, the more of the whole tapestry will appear both for us as teachers and for our students.

Short answer quiz

Instructions: Answer the following questions in 2-3 sentences each.

- Why can't we simply define verbs as action words? Use examples to support your answer.

- Explain how (e.g., walk) can be either a noun or a verb. What determines its category?

- What are the forms that most lexical verbs can take? What are some exceptions?

- Explain the difference between how adjective phrases and adverb phrases function as modifiers in relation to verbs.

- What is meant by agreement with subject for verbs? When does it occur?

- What distinguishes auxiliary verbs from other verbs? Give examples of two properties.

- How do verbs differ from nouns in terms of what they can denote? Why is this distinction important?

- What is the relationship between verbs, verb phrases, and clauses?

- What are defective verbs, and which forms do the examples in the chapter lack?

Answer key

- Many nouns denote actions (e.g., action, run, walk), while some verbs don't denote actions (e.g., exist, seem). The relationship between meaning and grammatical category is more complex than simple action/non-action.

- Words like walk (N) and walk (V) belong to the same word family but are different words. The verb takes verbal inflections and heads verb phrases; the noun takes determiners and inflects for number.

- Most lexical verbs have a plain form, an -s form, and an -ing form. In fact, there are a few other forms, but we'll cover those later.

- Adverb phrases commonly function as modifiers in VPs (run quickly), but adjective phrases don't. The situation is reversed in NPs, where we have (a quick run) but not *a quickly run.

- Agreement selects between the plain and third-person singular present-tense verb forms. It also selects between were and was in the past tense.

- Auxiliary verbs have the NICER properties, including the ability to form negations directly (will not) and participate in subject-auxiliary inversion (Has Lee eaten?). Regular verbs lack these properties.

- Verbs are chiefly limited to denoting actions and states, though they also denote time through tense. Nouns are not limited in this way.

- Verbs head verb phrases, which in turn head clauses. This hierarchical relationship is fundamental to English sentence structure.

- Defective verbs are those that lack one or more of the typical verb forms. The chapter gives examples like must, beware, and ought, which lack past-tense forms, and modal auxiliaries like can, may, will, shall, and must, which lack -s and -ing forms.

Discussion questions

- Discuss how formal properties (inflection, modification patterns) and meaning interact in the identification of nouns versus verbs in English. How does this understanding help us avoid oversimplified definitions like action words or person, place, or thing? Provide specific examples from the text.

- Explain how seemingly straightforward concepts like countability and plurality reveal the complex and often arbitrary nature of linguistic categories. Use examples from different languages to illustrate why these categories are inter-subjective judgments rather than objective facts.

- Analyze the relationship between verbs and auxiliary verbs in English. How do the NICER properties distinguish auxiliaries, and what implications does this have for English language teaching?

- Examine the hierarchical relationships among lexical and phrasal categories (for example, between nouns and noun phrases, or verbs, verb phrases, and clauses). How does understanding these technical relationships help teachers better analyze and explain grammatical structures?
